data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-01T01:43:28.089Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating a .NET 10 web API to .NET 11. The app uses EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider.
We have a repository pattern where some methods use synchronous LINQ:

```csharp
public class ProductRepository
{
    private readonly CosmosDbContext _context;

    public List<Product> GetAll()
    {
        return _context.Products.ToList();
    }

    public Product GetById(int id)
    {
        return _context.Products.First(p => p.Id == id);
    }

    public int GetCount()
    {
        return _context.Products.Count();
    }

    public void Save(Product product)
    {
        _context.Products.Add(product);
        _context.SaveChanges();
    }
}
```

In .NET 10 we had the sync-over-async opt-in configured. What breaks in .NET 11?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about breaking changes when migrating from .NET 10 to .NET 11, specifically regarding EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider and synchronous LINQ operations.

This is a knowledge question about EF Core's evolution. Let me think about what I know:

In EF Core with the Cosmos DB provider, there has been a long-running effort to remove synchronous APIs because the underlying Azure Cosmos DB SDK is async-only. The "sync-over-async" pattern (where sync methods internally call async methods and block) was considered an anti-pattern.

In EF Core 9 (with .NET 9), the Cosmos DB provider started throwing by default when synchronous APIs were used, but you could opt back in. The opt-in was via a warning configuration or a specific option.


Looking at the progression, EF Core 9 introduced exceptions for sync operations on Cosmos DB with an opt-in workaround available, EF Core 10 likely kept that opt-in but marked it as deprecated, and EF Core 11 would logically complete the removal by eliminating synchronous APIs entirely for the Cosmos DB provider. The fix would require converting all sync operations to their async equivalents—`ToListAsync()`, `FirstAsync()`, `CountAsync()`, `SaveChangesAsync()`—since the configuration option to suppress these errors would be removed entirely. I should note that .NET 11 is still in preview as of May 2026 and won't ship until November, so I'm extrapolating based on EF Core 9's known trajectory with the Cosmos provider, which already blocks sync I/O by default. I should structure my answer around what specifically breaks, which methods stop working, and how to migrate to async alternatives.
